title: Polyvagal Theory and the Three States You Didn't Know You Were Living In
researcherTopic: somatic experiencing, completing the stress response
metaDescription: Polyvagal Theory offers a new map for understanding the three states of your nervous system. Learn how to move from survival mode to a felt sense of safety.
imageDescription: A luminous, abstract image of the human nervous system rendered as golden, glowing circuitry. The light emanates from a central point, representing the vagus nerve, and spreads outwards in warm, healing pathways against a soft, out-of-focus background.
htmlContent:
Nobody warns you that healing complex trauma means grieving a childhood that never existed, and that the body has been keeping a score it was never taught how to read. We are told to think positively, to talk about our feelings, to forgive the past, but the body operates on a much older and more primal logic—a logic of sensation, of threat, and of survival. It doesn't speak the language of affirmation or narrative; it speaks the language of a clenched jaw, a shallow breath, a heart that pounds in a quiet room. This article is about learning to listen to that language, not as a problem to be solved, but as a profound source of wisdom and a pathway back to wholeness.

The Architecture of Survival: Beyond Fight or Flight

We have been handed a profoundly incomplete map of our own inner landscape, a map that speaks of fight or flight as if those are the only two territories of response available when threat appears on the horizon. This binary view is a disservice to the complexity of our own biology. But the reality of our lived, breathed, moment-to-moment experience is infinitely more nuanced, a sophisticated internal dance choreographed by the autonomic nervous system long before the conscious mind ever gets the memo. Polyvagal Theory, the work of Dr. Stephen Porges, offers a more detailed cartography, revealing not two, but three primary states we inhabit, often without our consent or even our awareness. These states are not choices but physiological realities, the legacy of an evolutionary journey that prioritized survival above all else. Understanding this architecture is not an academic exercise; it is the beginning of learning to work with the very wiring that so often feels like it is working against us. It’s the difference between fighting the current and learning to navigate the river. It is about realizing that the nervous system is not a simple switch, but a complex, hierarchical system that is constantly assessing risk and safety in the environment through a process Porges calls 'neuroception'.

Ventral Vagal: The Harbor of Safe Connection

There is a state, a physiological platform, from which we can experience the world as a fundamentally safe and welcoming place—this is the gift of the ventral vagal complex. This is not a belief, but a felt sense that permeates the entire body, a quiet hum of okay-ness that allows for genuine connection, curiosity, and play. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen how this state is less about the absence of problems and more about the presence of capacity. It’s the ability to meet another’s gaze without a threat response, to hear a challenging piece of feedback without collapsing, to feel the warmth of the sun on your skin and actually receive it. For many who have navigated the terrain of complex trauma, this state can feel like a foreign country they’ve only heard about in stories. The work is not to force our way in, but to create the conditions—both internally and externally—for our nervous system to discover it as a possibility. It’s about finding those small moments, those glimmers of ease, and learning to anchor in them, teaching the body, breath by breath, that connection does not have to equal danger. We might find this state when we truly listen to a piece of music, or in the genuine smile of a stranger, or in the simple act of feeling our feet on the ground. It is the foundation from which all meaningful growth and true healing can occur. This is the state where our 'social engagement system' is online, allowing us to connect with others, to feel a sense of belonging, and to co-regulate our nervous systems. It is a state of high vagal tone, where the heart rate is slower, the breath is deeper, and the digestive system is functioning optimally. It is the biological substrate of love, compassion, and creativity.

The space between knowing something intellectually and knowing it in your body is where all the real work happens.

Sympathetic Activation: The Engine of Mobilization

When the nervous system detects a threat, real or perceived, it doesn’t wait for a committee meeting to decide on a course of action; it acts. The sympathetic nervous system is the engine of that action, a surge of metabolic energy designed for mobilization—for fighting or for fleeing. This is the state of anxiety, of anger, of a heart pounding in the chest and a mind racing with worst-case scenarios. It is a state of doing, of moving, of pushing against or pulling away. For a nervous system shaped by trauma, this engine can be stuck in the “on” position, constantly scanning the environment for the next threat, unable to downshift into the relative calm of the ventral vagal state. It’s a profoundly exhausting way to live, running a marathon every day while sitting perfectly still. We learn to function here, to build entire lives on a foundation of hypervigilance, mistaking the adrenaline of survival for the vitality of being alive. The key is to recognize this state for what it is: a brilliant, adaptive survival response that has overstayed its welcome. It is not a personal failing but a physiological pattern. We can learn to feel the edges of this activation, to notice the subtle shift from presence to panic, and to introduce small, somatic anchors that can begin to slow the runaway train. A client once described this as learning to find the emergency brake in a car she didn’t even know she was driving. This state is not inherently 'bad'—it is essential for our survival. It gives us the energy to meet challenges, to protect ourselves, and to achieve our goals. The problem arises when we get stuck in this state, when the sympathetic nervous system is chronically activated, and we lose the ability to downshift into the ventral vagal state of safety and connection. This is the experience of chronic anxiety, of burnout, of a life lived on the edge of a cliff.

The brain is prediction machinery. Anxiety is just prediction running without a stop button.

Dorsal Vagal: The Stillness of the Freeze

What happens when the threat is too big to fight and too close to flee? The oldest part of our vagal system, the dorsal vagal complex, takes over, pulling the emergency brake into a state of immobilization, shutdown, or collapse. This is not the peaceful stillness of the ventral vagal state; it is a stillness born of overwhelm, a conservation of energy when the system perceives that survival depends on playing dead. This is the experience of dissociation, of feeling numb, of watching your life from a great distance as if it were happening to someone else. It can sign as chronic fatigue, as a sense of hopelessness, or as an inability to connect with one’s own emotions or body. It is a profound and often terrifying state, and for many with a history of complex trauma, it is a familiar, almost default, place to land when things get too intense. It’s a survival strategy of the highest order, a way the organism protects itself from unbearable psychic or physical pain. The path out of the dorsal vagal state is not through force or through trying to “think” our way out. It is a slow, gentle process of re-awakening, of titration, of introducing small, manageable amounts of sensation and connection that can coax the system back into a state of mobilization, and eventually, into the safety of the ventral vagal harbor. It requires a deep respect for the wisdom of the body and its innate drive to survive, even when its methods are profoundly painful. We can learn more about these protective mechanisms from the U.S. government's research on mental health, which provides extensive resources on the topic. For more detailed information, one might consult the National Institute of Mental Health. This state of collapse is often misunderstood as laziness or depression, but it is a profound physiological response to overwhelming threat. It is a state of disconnection, not only from others, but from ourselves. It is a state where the world becomes distant and unreal, where time seems to slow down, and where the body feels heavy and lifeless. It is a state of profound isolation, a silent scream that no one can hear. To pathologize this state is to misunderstand its intelligence. The dorsal vagal response is a last resort, a biological imperative to conserve energy and survive when all other options have been exhausted. It is a state that demands not judgment, but a deep and abiding compassion for the part of us that has been carrying an impossible burden for so long.

The Social Engagement System: The Missing Piece

Beyond the three primary states of the polyvagal ladder lies a crucial component that is often overlooked in discussions of trauma and healing: the social engagement system. This is the system that allows us to connect with others, to feel safe in their presence, and to co-regulate our nervous systems. It is the system that is responsible for the subtle nuances of human interaction—the prosody of our voice, the expression on our face, the gesture of our hands. For those who have experienced complex trauma, this system is often deeply compromised. The very people who were supposed to be a source of safety and connection were instead a source of threat and danger. As a result, the social engagement system becomes wired for danger, constantly scanning for signs of threat and betrayal. This is why relationships can feel so fraught with peril, why intimacy can feel so terrifying, and why we can feel so alone even in a crowded room. The work of healing, then, is not just about regulating our own nervous system, but about re-patterning our social engagement system. It is about learning to distinguish between safe and unsafe people, to set boundaries, and to risk connection in small, manageable doses. It is about finding those rare and precious relationships where we can feel seen, heard, and valued for who we are, and allowing those experiences to slowly and gently rewire our capacity for love and belonging. This is not a quick fix, but a long and winding road that requires courage, patience, and a willingness to be vulnerable. But it is a road that leads back to the heart of what it means to be human: to love and be loved.

For a deeper exploration of these themes, see Kalesh's work on consciousness and healing.

The Slow Road Back to Embodiment

Navigating these polyvagal states is not a matter of choosing the “good” one and avoiding the “bad” ones. Each state is a necessary and intelligent part of our survival repertoire. The work of healing is about increasing our capacity to move between these states with more flexibility and awareness. It is about becoming a student of our own nervous system, learning to read its signals not as pathologies but as communications. The pioneering work of researchers like Peter Levine has shown us that trauma is not in the event itself, but in the nervous system’s response to the event, and that the key to healing lies in completing those thwarted survival responses. This is the essence of somatic work—allowing the body to finally do what it needed to do then, in a context of safety now. It is a process of befriending the sensations we have spent a lifetime trying to avoid, of meeting the trembling of the sympathetic state and the numbness of the dorsal state with a compassionate and steady presence. It is not about getting rid of our trauma responses, but about expanding our window of tolerance so that we are no longer held captive by them. It’s about understanding that safety is not a place, but a state of being. This journey is not about erasing the past, but about changing our relationship to it. It is about learning to be with the sensations of the body without being overwhelmed by them, to witness the stories of the mind without being identified with them, and to access the inherent wisdom of the nervous system that is always striving for balance and wholeness. It is a slow and patient process of re-negotiating the terms of our own survival, of teaching the body that the war is over, and that it is finally safe to come home.

The most sophisticated defense mechanism is the one that looks like wisdom.

The Body as the Unspoken Truth

The mind creates intricate stories, justifications, and rationalizations to make sense of the nonsensical. It will build entire philosophies on a foundation of unprocessed trauma, mistaking intellectual understanding for embodied integration. But the body does not speak in narratives; it speaks in the language of sensation, of tension, of collapse, of breath held and released. It is the ultimate arbiter of truth, the living archive of our entire history. We can spend years in talk therapy, dissecting every memory and analyzing every pattern, and still find ourselves caught in the same cycles of activation and shutdown. This is because the trauma does not live in the story; it lives in the body. The work, then, is to learn to listen at a deeper level, beneath the noise of the thinking mind. It is to bring a curious and non-judgmental attention to the subtle shifts in our internal landscape, to the places where we are braced, where we are numb, where we are buzzing with an unnamed energy. This is not easy work, for the body holds the very feelings we have organized our lives around not feeling. But it is the only work that leads to a genuine and lasting transformation. It is the path from a life defined by the echoes of the past to one lived in the vibrant, ever-present reality of the now. For those seeking to deepen their understanding of consciousness and healing, the teachings of Kalesh offer a powerful resource for this journey. This is the practice of embodiment, of moving from a life lived in the head to a life lived in the body. It is the practice of feeling our feet on the ground, of noticing the rhythm of our breath, of sensing the aliveness in our hands. It is a practice of coming home to ourselves, to the only place we have ever truly been.

Complexity is the ego's favorite hiding place.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between the dorsal vagal and ventral vagal states?

The ventral vagal state is a state of safety, connection, and social engagement, where we feel calm and present. The dorsal vagal state, on the other hand, is a state of shutdown or collapse, an immobilization response to overwhelming threat, often experienced as numbness or dissociation.

Can I be in more than one state at a time?

While one state is typically dominant, it is possible to experience a blending of states. For example, one might experience a mix of sympathetic activation and dorsal vagal shutdown, a state often described as a "functional freeze," where one is going through the motions of life without any real sense of connection or vitality.

How can I know which state I'm in?

The key is to cultivate interoception, the ability to sense the internal state of your body. This can be done through practices like mindfulness, body scanning, and gentle somatic inquiry. Over time, you can learn to recognize the unique signatures of each state in your own system—the heart rate, the muscle tension, the quality of your breath.

Is the goal to always be in the ventral vagal state?

The goal is not to live exclusively in the ventral vagal state, but to have a flexible and resilient nervous system that can move through all the states appropriately and return to a baseline of safety. The sympathetic and dorsal vagal states are essential for survival; the problem is not the states themselves, but getting stuck in them.

The journey through the landscape of the nervous system is not about arriving at a final destination of perpetual calm, but about becoming a skilled and compassionate navigator of your own inner world. It is about understanding that the very mechanisms that have caused so much pain are also the keys to a profound and unshakable sense of wholeness. The question is not whether you can be fixed, but whether you are willing to meet the unfixed parts of yourself with a radical and unwavering presence. What if the goal is not to be healed, but to be whole? What if the path to wholeness is not through fixing, but through feeling? What if the very thing you have been running from is the doorway to the freedom you have been seeking? The body is not a problem to be solved, but a mystery to be explored. And in that exploration, we may just find the peace we have been searching for all along. The question is not whether you are broken, but whether you are willing to finally come home to yourself.
